answer: He wanted to show kinbaku’s distinctive tempestuousness without modification.
evidence: I interviewed the photographer Yasui Kissyou san. People usually retouch portraits in order to hide wrinkles, blemishes, Etc., but amazingly, he did no retouching at all to the photos for this exhibition. He said because he wanted to express the distinctive tempestuousness of Kinbaku without modification. So it is indeed very real. I was even able to very clearly see Tatami marks on thighs and downy hair on breasts. It is amazing that models accept this, and must be a result of the relationship of mutual trust.
expected_answer: he wanted to express the distinctive tempestuousness of Kinbaku without modification
expected_question: What reason did photographer Yasui Kissyou san give for not retouching the portraits?
paraphrase_rationale: The revised question names the exhibition and the answer is a grammatical normalization of the photographer’s stated artistic reason.
question: Why did photographer Yasui Kissyou leave the Kinbaku Shashuu portraits unretouched?
reason: The revised question names the exhibition and the answer is a grammatical normalization of the photographer’s stated artistic reason.

answer: He continually questioned whether each choice was best for ease of tying, connection, aesthetics, and other considerations.
evidence: For me the best thing about Osada sensei is his constant questioning and not doing anything because he was told but because it is the best thing to do based on a range of considerations (ease of tying, connection, aesthetics etc). For this reason his rope and focus is always in flux, and each time I return to Tokyo things have changed. Whilst on my first visit his teaching was more technical, on subsequent visits it was more about connection and newaza. Ultimately Osada Steve is trying to share his great love of Shibari/Kinabku, and therefore is generous with his knowledge. It is something that I try to emulate.
expected_answer: ease of tying, connection, aesthetics etc
expected_question: What specific considerations does Osada Steve use to determine the best course of action when tying?
paraphrase_rationale: The revised Q&A captures the interview’s transferable lesson—continual critical questioning—instead of asking for an incomplete list ending in ‘etc.’
question: According to Scott, what habit kept Osada Steve’s rope and teaching focus changing over time?
reason: The revised Q&A captures the interview’s transferable lesson—continual critical questioning—instead of asking for an incomplete list ending in ‘etc.’

answer: resentment that has congealed into a durable, almost haunting force when injury cannot be resolved through ordinary justice
evidence: The section title of the sixth section “A Wife’s Resentment” (女房の怨念) is crucial because onnen (怨念) does not simply name anger or resentment. It names resentment that has congealed into a durable, almost haunting force. In the logic of Japanese ghost stories, onnen is what remains when injury cannot be resolved through ordinary justice. Here, Otori’s grief over her husband’s destruction and Umeko’s suffering under Iwazō’s debt violence become part of the same moral atmosphere: the wronged wife’s resentment gathers until it demands repayment.
expected_question: What specific Japanese term names resentment that has congealed into a durable, almost haunting force, remaining when injury cannot be resolved through ordinary justice?
paraphrase_rationale: The revised Q&A teaches the article’s definition directly instead of requiring the learner to retrieve a rare Japanese term from a long definition.
question: In Kinbaku Today’s “The Ghosts of Kinbaku History,” what does onnen mean?
reason: The revised Q&A teaches the article’s definition directly instead of requiring the learner to retrieve a rare Japanese term from a long definition.
